defects_verified.title: Players with zero tracked data at a given time render nothing — no skeleton, no mesh, no floor marker, no placeholder
defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: Player render logic (App.tsx Player component) has exactly three tiers — real joint skeleton, generic proxy stick-figure, solid mesh — and all three require at least one populated pose field (joints_world, floor_world_xyz, or track_world_xy) on the nearest-time world frame. When upstream tracking/pose has a real gap (the frame object exists but every pose field is null), none of the tiers can produce geometry, so the player is 100% invisible with no 'off-screen'/'lost track' indicator at all.
defects_verified.evidence: web/replay/src/App.tsx:2139-2200 (Player), App.tsx:3079-3117 (floorWorldForFrame/skeletonForFrame both return null with no data); confirmed against real data: player 20 in runs/lanes/w7_critique_20260709/wolv_world/wolverine_mixed_0200_mid_steep_corner/confidence_gated_world.json has 93 consecutive null-pose frames (3.10s) at clip start out of a 10s clip; visually reproduced in runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/qa_crop.png (only 3 of 4 players' meshes visible)
defects_verified.fix_sketch: Add a fourth tier in Player: when frame exists but has no pose data (or frame is fully absent within the player's temporal_coverage span), render an explicit low-opacity 'no detection this instant' placeholder (e.g. dashed silhouette or floor ring at last-known position) instead of nothing, and surface a per-player coverage gap indicator in the header stat row.

votes.reasoning:
Verified the two cited code regions against current HEAD. App.tsx:2114-2201 (Player component) does exactly what the claim describes for the floor-marker and skeleton tiers: floorWorldForFrame() (App.tsx:3079-3081) and bodyJointSkeletonForFrame()/skeletonForFrame() (App.tsx:3083-3113, 3168-3199) all return null when frame.joints_world is empty and frame.floor_world_xyz/track_world_xy are null, which is confirmed to occur in real data (player 20 in wolverine_mixed_0200_mid_steep_corner/confidence_gated_world.json has frames with joints_world=[], floor_world_xyz=null, track_world_xy=null, tagged trust_band.gate_id='world_no_data_placeholder'). No 'lost track'/'off-screen' UI text exists anywhere in App.tsx (grep confirmed). So the floor+skeleton-tier portion of the claim is accurate.

However, the claim's root_cause_hypothesis is factually wrong about the third tier: 'solid mesh' is NOT part of the Player component and does NOT require any populated pose field on the nearest-time world frame. It is rendered by a separate component (SolidBodyMeshes/SolidBodyMesh, App.tsx:2217-2399) driven by solidBodyMeshFramesForTime() (viewerData.ts:1214-1252), which resolves the mesh player via normalizedMeshPlayerIdForWorldFrame() — this falls back to player.id even when the world frame is entirely undefined (viewerData.ts:2693-2695) — and then pulls geometry from the BodyMesh player's own independent frame timeline via bodyMeshFrameForTime() (viewerData.ts:2097-2124), which has its own hold/fade tolerance (BODY_MESH_FADE_SECONDS=0.12s) fully decoupled from joints_world/floor_world_xyz/track_world_xy. alignBodyMeshFrameToWorldSkeleton() (viewerData.ts:2302-2324) explicitly handles a missing world frame by rendering the mesh unaligned rather than suppressing it. So a real world-pose gap does not by itself blank the solid mesh; mesh disappearance (visually corroborated in qa_crop.png, showing only 3 of 4 players' meshes, tagged 'mesh: computed 30fps...') is caused by a coverage gap in the BodyMesh pipeline's own frame array, an architecturally separate and independently-sourced failure mode. The claim conflates two independent systems into one mechanism, and its own citation range (App.tsx:2139-2200) doesn't even include the SolidBodyMesh code (2217-2399) it is describing. Consequently the fix_sketch ('add a fourth tier inside Player') would not fix mesh-tier invisibility at all, since that render path lives entirely outside Player and needs its own no-coverage placeholder logic in SolidBodyMeshes/solidBodyMeshFramesForTime instead.

Both the code claim and the artifact evidence check out. Code: web/replay/src/viewerData.ts:1095 frameForTime() does pure nearest-timestamp lookup within a player's frame range with no check on whether any pose field is populated — it will happily return a frame whose floor_world_xyz/track_world_xy/joints_world are all null. In web/replay/src/App.tsx the Player component's floor marker (floorWorldForFrame, line ~3079), proxy stick-figure (skeletonForFrame, requires floorWorldForFrame), and real skeleton (bodyJointSkeletonForFrame, requires joints_world) all correctly return null/empty in that case, so none of the three geometry paths render anything — confirmed by direct inspection of App.tsx:2139-2200 and the floorWorldForFrame/skeletonForFrame definitions. I also confirmed the only existing gap-indicator (coverageGapActive / playerCoverageStats in viewerData.ts:1376) aggregates raw frame timestamps across ALL players and only fires after the very last frame's t globally — it does not detect per-player, mid-clip null-pose runs, so the claim that there is "no lost-track indicator at all" for this scenario is accurate, not overstated.

Artifact evidence also checks out exactly as cited: runs/lanes/w7_critique_20260709/wolv_world/wolverine_mixed_0200_mid_steep_corner/confidence_gated_world.json player id 20 has exactly 93 consecutive frames (t=1.867s to 4.933s, ~3.1s) with floor_world_xyz, track_world_xy, and joints_world all empty/null — verified by parsing the JSON directly. Going further than the claim, the same defect is not an isolated edge case: in the demo render's own data (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/after_wolv/confidence_gated_world.json) three of four players have multiple mid-clip null-pose runs of 40+ frames (~1.4s), not just at clip start. The cited screenshot runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/qa_crop.png does show only 3 of 4 players rendered (two players with paddles at the baseline plus one at the net; the near-camera court half is empty), consistent with a player having zero renderable geometry at that instant.

Nothing in the artifacts contradicts the claim; if anything the demo data shows the gap problem is worse/more frequent (mid-clip, not just startup) than the single cited example suggests. This is a real, reproducible, currently-unmitigated defect, not a stale or mis-attributed one.

defects_verified.title: Measured live '3D FPS' render-loop stat reads 1.3-3.5fps in the actual demo footage
defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: FpsProbe measures the real react-three-fiber/useFrame render loop (Canvas defaults to frameloop='always'), so this is a genuine rendered-frames-per-second number, not a data-cadence estimate. Candidate causes: (a) heavy per-currentTime-change recomputation — solidBodyMeshFramesForTime, alignBodyMeshFrameToWorldSkeleton, createSolidBodyMeshGeometryCache and multiple other useMemo hooks all key off `currentTime`, which updates on every native video timeupdate; (b) the screenshots were captured via a headless/automated capture pipeline (per runs/lanes/demo_beststack_20260710/REPORT.md section 3), which may run without GPU acceleration (software WebGL), inflating the apparent slowness beyond what a real device would show. Both need to be separated with a live-browser profile before assigning a fix.
defects_verified.evidence: web/replay/src/App.tsx:1668-1688 (FpsProbe), App.tsx:1208-1217 (Canvas config), App.tsx:1051 (Metric tile); runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/frames_after_world/screenshot_t2s.png (1.3fps) and screenshot_t5p4s.png (3.5fps)
defects_verified.fix_sketch: Profile the Canvas render loop in a real (non-headless, GPU-accelerated) browser session on the same clip; if CPU-bound, memoize/throttle the heaviest per-frame recomputations (mesh alignment, geometry cache rebuilds) so they run at data cadence rather than every rAF tick; if capture-environment-bound, exclude this number from being read as representative and re-measure on-device before treating it as the S1 root cause.

votes.reasoning: All cited evidence verifies against current HEAD. FpsProbe (App.tsx ~1675-1685) samples the real react-three-fiber useFrame render loop; Canvas (App.tsx ~1208-1219) has no frameloop override so defaults to r3f's continuous "always" mode (confirmed via grep — no frameloop prop anywhere in App.tsx); the "3D FPS" Metric tile (line 1051) reads that sampled fps state. The two cited screenshots (screenshot_t2s.png, screenshot_t5p4s.png) literally display "3D FPS 1.3" and "3D FPS 3.5" in the HUD, confirmed by viewing the images directly. Hypothesis (a) — currentTime-keyed useMemo recomputation — checks out: activeBodyMeshes/solidBodyMeshFramesForTime (line 841) depends on currentTime, and currentTime is set from the native video's currentTime on time-update events (lines 814/942); one minor looseness is that createSolidBodyMeshGeometryCache (line 845) is actually keyed only on renderBodyMesh, not currentTime, so "multiple other useMemo hooks... key off currentTime" over-includes that one example, but it's incidental to the claim's substance. Hypothesis (b) is more strongly supported than the claim even asserts: scripts/racketsport/verify_process_video_viewer.py launches Playwright with `p.chromium.launch(headless=True)` and zero GPU-related flags (no --use-gl/--use-angle/--enable-gpu), and runs/lanes/demo_beststack_20260710/REPORT.md section 3 confirms these exact screenshots came from "live headless viewer captures" — this is a real, uninstrumented headless-Chromium capture path, not a hypothetical. The claim is explicitly hedged (presents two candidate causes, states they must be separated via live-browser profiling before assigning a fix) rather than overstated as a definitive root cause. Nothing here is stale, already-fixed, or mis-attributed to the wrong file/lines.

All load-bearing claims check out against real artifacts, with exact-match precision:

1. FPS values: screenshot_t2s.png shows the on-screen '3D FPS' tile reading exactly 1.3, and screenshot_t5p4s.png reads exactly 3.5 -- verified by direct visual inspection of the PNGs in runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/frames_after_world/. headless_verify.json in the same dir also independently logs "3D FPS": "1.2" for the first-load screenshot.

2. Code citations are exact, line-for-line: App.tsx:1668-1688 is FpsProbe (uses useFrame -- the real R3F/rAF render loop -- and updateFpsSample, a correct rolling ~500ms fps counter with no bug of its own); App.tsx:1208-1217 is the Canvas config (dpr [1.5,2], gl powerPreference high-performance/antialias, no `frameloop` prop set, so it defaults to R3F's 'always' -- confirmed no `frameloop` string appears anywhere in App.tsx); App.tsx:1051 is the Metric tile rendering `fps.toFixed(1)`. So the claim's framing -- this is a genuine rendered-frames-per-second number, not a data-cadence estimate -- is correct.

3. The headless-capture hypothesis (b) is strongly corroborated, not speculative: scripts/racketsport/verify_process_video_viewer.py:331 does `p.chromium.launch(headless=True)` with no GPU-enabling flags (no --use-gl/--enable-gpu/--use-angle), and this exact script is what produced headless_verify.json and every screenshot_t*.png in the cited run directory (confirmed via the 'loaded ... headless' note in headless_verify.json). Default headless Chromium launches typically run WebGL through software rendering (SwiftShader) rather than real GPU acceleration, which is a well-known cause of exactly this kind of render-loop slowdown -- so hypothesis (b) is not just plausible, it is the more likely dominant explanation given the artifacts.

4. The claim's link to 'actual demo footage' is not an exaggeration: runs/lanes/demo_beststack_20260710/REPORT.md section 3 ('Demo video') states explicitly the delivered demo video was 'Assembled from: live headless viewer captures (before/after worlds, world view)... Assembly evidence in main checkout runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/' -- i.e. these exact headless screenshots (with the low FPS tile visible in-frame) fed the video delivered to the owner's Desktop, not just an internal QA-only artifact.

5. The claim is appropriately hedged: it does not assert which of (a) or (b) is the true root cause, explicitly says 'Both need to be separated with a live-browser profile before assigning a fix,' and the fix_sketch matches that (profile in a real GPU-accelerated browser first). This is honest engineering, not overclaiming.

Nothing in the evidence contradicts the claim; every citation resolves to real, matching content. The only soft point is the 'critical' severity label (a viewer-only cosmetic/optics stat, not a data-correctness defect), but severity calibration alone does not meet the bar for 'wrong, overstated, already fixed, or mis-attributed' when the underlying technical claim, evidence, and hedging are all sound.
votes.corrected_root_cause: Not a correction, a refinement: given verify_process_video_viewer.py launches Chromium via Playwright with headless=True and no GPU-enabling flags, hypothesis (b) (software/SwiftShader rendering in the capture pipeline) is the more probable dominant contributor to the 1.2-3.5fps readings, with hypothesis (a) (heavy currentTime-keyed useMemo recomputation) a plausible secondary/compounding factor still worth profiling separately, exactly as the claim recommends.

defects_verified.title: Fail-closed ball emission (rev 11) hides ~75-78% of ball frames in the demo clip with no distinct '3D predicted/hidden' marker
defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: The Ball 3D component only has 3 states (solid dot / 2D-only ghost ring / nothing). ballFrameForTime and videoBallOverlayForTime both drop visible===false frames identically, so on a fail-closed-hidden frame there is no 3D dot, no 2D overlay dot, and no ghost — the sole surviving signal is a small text HUD chip reading 'ball: not visible'. Combined with the fail-closed policy landed same-day (best_stack rev 11) suppressing 235/300 frames in this clip, the net effect the owner sees is a ball that is absent far more often than present, with no strong visual cue explaining why.
defects_verified.evidence: web/replay/src/App.tsx:2572-2597 (Ball/BallGhostMarkerRing); web/replay/src/viewerData.ts:1103-1127 (both filters read visible!==false); web/replay/src/components/modules/BallHonestyHud.tsx:30-65 (text-only fallback); runs/lanes/demo_beststack_20260710/REPORT.md section 1 table (300->75 emitted, 224 hidden bands); runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/frames_after_world/screenshot_t2s.png ('54/300 measured · 11 predicted · 235 hidden')
defects_verified.fix_sketch: Give the hidden-ball state (as distinct from the existing '2D-only' ghost mode) its own visible-but-clearly-marked 3D indicator — e.g. a low-opacity dashed sphere at the last known/physics-extrapolated position, colored/labeled as not-authoritative — rather than rendering nothing; this is also what NORTH_STAR section 1.4's trust contract calls for ('missing' should still be visually accounted for, and 'physics_predicted' must be visibly distinguished).

votes.reasoning:
Traced the actual live render tree in web/replay/src/App.tsx. The functions the claim cites as the ball's 3D rendering path — `Ball` and `BallGhostMarkerRing` (App.tsx:2572-2597) — are DEAD CODE: `function Ball(...)` is defined once and never instantiated anywhere (`grep -n "<Ball " web/replay/src` returns nothing; it isn't exported or referenced by any JSX, test, or other module). The scene actually mounted in the Canvas (App.tsx:1263) is `<BallTrailLayer .../>`, backed by web/replay/src/components/modules/ballTrail.ts, not by ballFrameForTime/videoBallOverlayForTime from viewerData.ts (those two functions instead feed a 2D video-overlay dot (`VideoBallOverlayMark`, App.tsx:1168) and a text readout (`ballRenderText`, App.tsx:888) — separate UI, not the 3D ball). So the claim's premise 'ballFrameForTime and videoBallOverlayForTime both drop visible===false frames identically' is true as a code fact but irrelevant to the 3D-world ball, and the claim's 'Ball 3D component only has 3 states' is false of the code that actually runs.

The real 3D rendering system (ballTrail.ts:160-270, `styleForBand`) already implements exactly what the fix_sketch asks for: six distinct bands (anchored_measured, arc_interpolated, arc_extrapolated, arc_weak, hidden, unknown) each with its own color/opacity/line-pattern/pulsing treatment. 'measured' renders a solid yellow (#e8ff34) non-pulsing ball+trail; the three 'predicted' bands (interpolated/extrapolated/weak) each render a distinctly-colored (cyan/blue/orange), dashed, PULSING ball+trail (pulsingBall:true, rendersBall:true) — i.e. predicted-vs-measured is already visually distinguished in the 3D scene, contrary to the claim. Only the genuinely 'hidden' band (fail-closed-suppressed, no trustworthy signal at all) renders nothing in 3D (opacity 0, rendersBall/rendersTrail false) — but even that state gets a dedicated HUD chip (BallHonestyHud.tsx) with both a colored indicator dot (grey for not_visible vs dashed-cyan for predicted vs solid-yellow for measured) and a text label ('ball: not visible'), not a bare text string as claimed.

So the parts of the claim that survive are narrow and much less severe than stated: fail-closed-hidden frames (the true 'nothing to show' case, ~235/300 in the demo clip per REPORT.md) do render nothing in the 3D world besides the small HUD chip — but the predicted/interpolated/extrapolated case the fix_sketch worries about is already handled with a distinct pulsing marker. The claim as written misattributes the mechanism to unused/dead code, overstates the state model ('only 3 states'), and asks for a fix (distinguish 'physics_predicted' visually) that is already implemented for every band except the deliberately-blank fail-closed 'hidden' case.
votes.corrected_root_cause: The cited App.tsx:2572-2597 `Ball`/`BallGhostMarkerRing` components are dead code (never mounted in JSX). The live 3D ball rendering path is `BallTrailLayer` (App.tsx:1263) / web/replay/src/components/modules/ballTrail.ts, which already gives 'predicted' bands (arc_interpolated/arc_extrapolated/arc_weak) a distinct pulsing, dashed, uniquely-colored marker separate from the solid-yellow 'measured' ball. Only the truly fail-closed 'hidden' band renders nothing in the 3D scene by design (rendersBall/rendersTrail=false, opacity 0), falling back to BallHonestyHud's colored-dot + text chip ('ball: not visible'). If there is a real gap, it is narrower than claimed: whether the deliberately-blank 'hidden' state should get some low-opacity 3D placeholder too — not that predicted/hidden are visually undifferentiated (they already are differentiated for every state except genuinely-hidden).

votes.reasoning: The claim's central code evidence is dead code. `Ball`/`BallGhostMarkerRing` (App.tsx:2572-2620) are defined but never instantiated anywhere in the JSX render tree — grep across web/replay/src confirms zero `<Ball .../>` or `<BallGhostMarkerRing .../>` usages; git blame shows this function predates the ball-chain feature and was superseded. The component actually wired into the Canvas (App.tsx:1262-1272) is `BallTrailLayer` (components/modules/BallTrailLayer.tsx), driven by `styleForBand()` in ballTrail.ts. That live pipeline already implements the exact fix the claim asks for: a 'measured' state (solid yellow sphere, solid trail) is visually distinct from 'predicted' sub-bands (arc_weak/arc_extrapolated/arc_interpolated/unknown), each rendered as a dashed trail plus a pulsing translucent wireframe shell (`PulsingShell`) around the ball, in three different colors (orange #ffb454, blue #78b7ff, cyan #63d9ff) — i.e. a 'low-opacity, clearly marked, not-authoritative' 3D indicator, which is precisely the claim's own fix_sketch. The live demo screenshot (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/frames_after_world/screenshot_t2s.png) confirms the coverage HUD explicitly separates '54/300 measured · 11 predicted · 235 hidden' — a 3-way split the code backs with 3 distinct visual treatments, not the 2-state (dot/ghost) system the claim describes. Additionally, a second HUD (BallHonestyHud, bottom-left of the 3D pane) gives a persistent colored-dot + text readout ('ball: measured' / 'ball: predicted' / 'ball: not visible') for every frame, so the 'sole surviving signal' claim is also inaccurate — there are two independent honesty signals (top-right numeric counter + bottom-left state chip), not one. The only accurate residual: for genuinely zero-confidence ('hidden' band) frames, `styleForBand` sets opacity 0 / rendersBall false, so nothing is drawn in the 3D scene — but this is a defensible, already-flagged fail-closed design (no fabricated position for data with zero confidence), not the 'no distinct predicted marker' defect described, since predicted frames already get a very distinct marker. The claim mis-attributes the gap to the wrong (unused) component and overstates the absence of any visual differentiation.
votes.corrected_root_cause: The viewer's active ball-render path (BallTrailLayer + styleForBand) already gives 'predicted' frames a visually distinct marker (dashed trail, pulsing translucent shell, band-specific color) separate from 'measured' (solid yellow) and provides two honest-coverage HUDs (top-right numeric measured/predicted/hidden counter; bottom-left BallHonestyHud state chip). The code the claim cites as the culprit (App.tsx `Ball`/`BallGhostMarkerRing`, viewerData.ts `ballFrameForTime`/`videoBallOverlayForTime`) is dead code not reachable from the render tree. The only real, narrower gap is that fully 'hidden' (zero-confidence) frames intentionally render nothing in 3D — a fail-closed design choice already surfaced via the two text/numeric HUDs — not an unmarked 'predicted' state.

defects_verified.title: Paddle geometry is 100% wrist-anchored procedural proxy, not a detected paddle pose, for every player in the demo clip
defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: isEstimatedPaddleFrame() returns true whenever paddle.trust_band.badge !== 'verified'; in the real demo world every one of the 4 tracked players' paddle frames carries trust_band.badge='low_confidence', gate_id='wrist_proxy_estimated_paddle'. The renderer then falls into paddleRenderGeometryForFrame's box branch: a generic rounded-rectangle plate built from paddle_dims_in and positioned/oriented purely by pose_se3.t/R, which is itself derived from a wrist joint, not a detected paddle. The viewer is rendering exactly what the pipeline hands it, but does so as a solid-looking amber plate with a face-normal stick rather than something that reads as clearly provisional.
defects_verified.evidence: web/replay/src/viewerData.ts:1277-1285 (isEstimatedPaddleFrame); web/replay/src/App.tsx:3248-3336 (paddleRenderGeometryForFrame/paddleRenderMaterial); runs/lanes/w7_critique_20260709/wolv_world/wolverine_mixed_0200_mid_steep_corner/confidence_gated_world.json paddles[].trust_band (all 4 low_confidence/wrist_proxy_estimated_paddle); visual confirmation in runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/qa_crop.png
defects_verified.fix_sketch: This is jointly a pipeline gap (no promoted true-6DoF paddle detector feeding this clip class) and a viewer presentation gap. Viewer-side: make the wrist-proxy paddle render far more visually distinct from a 'real' paddle (e.g. thin outline/wireframe only, explicit 'estimated position' label near the entity) instead of a solid filled plate, so it reads as a hint rather than a rendered result users judge as wrong geometry.

votes.reasoning: Every load-bearing citation checks out against current HEAD: isEstimatedPaddleFrame() at viewerData.ts:1277-1285 matches verbatim; the referenced confidence_gated_world.json shows all 4 tracked players' paddles with trust_band.badge='low_confidence' and gate_id='wrist_proxy_estimated_paddle'; paddleRenderGeometryForFrame/paddleRenderMaterial at App.tsx:3248-3336 exist as described and give the 'estimated' state only a minor color/opacity difference (amber #ffb454 @0.84 vs green #e8ff34 @0.88), with no wireframe or text-label distinction anywhere in PaddleProxy (App.tsx:2270-2341); qa_crop.png visually confirms solid amber plates with cyan normal sticks on every player. One secondary detail in the root_cause_hypothesis is imprecise: the claim says rendering falls into paddleRenderGeometryForFrame's TS-side 'box' fallback (roundedRectanglePoints, App.tsx:3286-3305), but that branch requires mesh_vertices_world.length<=4, and every real paddle frame carries 16 mesh vertices / 24 faces (populated unconditionally by _paddle_mesh_vertices_world in threed/racketsport/virtual_world.py:1604,1718-1758), so the actual code path taken is the pre-baked-mesh branch (App.tsx:3275-3285). That upstream Python mesh is itself a procedural wrist-anchored box built from paddle_dims_in and pose_se3.t/R, so the substantive defect (a procedurally-generated, non-detected paddle rendered as an undifferentiated solid plate) and the proposed fix location (paddleRenderMaterial/PaddleProxy, which applies identically regardless of which geometry branch fires) are unaffected by this correction.
votes.corrected_root_cause: The estimated-vs-verified gating (isEstimatedPaddleFrame) and the flat, minimally-differentiated material treatment (paddleRenderMaterial) are correctly cited and are the real cause of the 'looks like a detected paddle' problem. However, the geometry itself does not come from the viewer's local roundedRectanglePoints 'box branch' (that TS fallback is unreachable given current data — every paddle frame has 16 mesh_vertices_world/24 mesh_faces). It comes from a server-side procedural mesh (_paddle_mesh_vertices_world in threed/racketsport/virtual_world.py) built purely from paddle_dims_in and pose_se3.t/R (itself wrist-derived), baked into the JSON and rendered via App.tsx's mesh_vertices_world branch (lines 3275-3285) instead of the box branch (lines 3286-3305). The fix should target paddleRenderMaterial/PaddleProxy presentation, not the geometry-branch selection logic.

(1) web/replay/src/viewerData.ts:1277-1285 exactly matches the quoted isEstimatedPaddleFrame() logic, including the `paddle.trust_band?.badge !== "verified"` condition. (2) runs/lanes/w7_critique_20260709/wolv_world/wolverine_mixed_0200_mid_steep_corner/confidence_gated_world.json confirms all 4 tracked paddles (player_id 19,20,21,22) carry trust_band.badge='low_confidence', gate_id='wrist_proxy_estimated_paddle', gate_status='estimated_from_wrist', and frame.source='wrist_palm_grip_fused:court_Z0' for every frame -- i.e. every paddle in this demo world is wrist-proxy/non-verified, exactly as claimed. This is a minor implementation-detail error, not a defect-invalidating one, and does not change severity, symptom, or fix direction.

The renderer then draws a solid amber plate + cyan normal stick with no distinguishing provisional label (App.tsx paddleRenderMaterial/paddleRenderGeometryForFrame), visually confirmed in qa_crop.png. defects_verified.title: Zero-margin court-membership filter drops the majority of real player detections near lines/baseline
defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: The initial build_tracks() court filter (the one that actually determines who makes tracks.json before any refinement) uses court_margin_m=0.0 by default, i.e. an exact pickleball court rectangle with zero run-off tolerance. Pickleball players legitimately stand/move behind the baseline (serves, deep returns, retreating for lobs) and lunge past the sidelines during real rallies -- all outside this exact rectangle -- so every such frame is classified as a non-court detection and thrown away, even though the player was correctly detected and tracked by BoT-SORT.
defects_verified.evidence: scripts/racketsport/track.py:159 + threed/racketsport/person_fast.py:39-49 (zero-margin path = classify_point exact-polygon test) + threed/racketsport/court_zones.py:19-28 (pickleball 'court' zone has no buffer); empirically 1171/2048 (57%) of person detections dropped as outside_court in the demo-lane run that produced dinkvision_demo_v1/v2/v3.mp4 (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/after_wolv/metrics.json)
defects_verified.fix_sketch: Raise the default margin for the primary build_tracks court filter (currently the association-refinement profiles already use 1.0-2.0m for the same problem -- process_video.py RawPoolGlobalAssociationProfile default court_margin_m=2.0) so the first-pass filter doesn't need to rely on a possibly-inert second stage. Needs re-validation against the '0 spectator/far-off-court FP' gate, likely paired with a height/appearance heuristic rather than margin alone.

This produces a 'loose-pool' tracks.json. The claim's fix_sketch correctly notes a refinement stage exists (raw-pool global association, default profile court_margin_m=2.0, drawn from the SAME raw_tracked_detections.json before the zero-margin filter is applied) that normally overwrites tracks.json with a tolerant result. However, in the exact demo lane the claim cites as evidence, PIPELINE_SUMMARY.json shows that refinement was SKIPPED because the --reid-model OSNet checkpoint was not found at the expected path, so the pipeline silently fell back to keeping the zero-margin loose-pool tracks.json as final -- which is exactly what the 1171/2048-dropped metrics.json reflects. So the claim is real and reproducible, not stale or already-fixed, but its root-cause framing is incomplete/slightly mis-attributed: build_tracks()'s zero margin is not normally 'the filter that determines who makes tracks.json' in the healthy default pipeline -- it is superseded by a court_margin_m=2.0 refinement pass that runs by default. The demonstrated 57% drop in the cited run was proximately caused by that refinement pass being silently skipped (missing reid-model checkpoint on that host/lane), which let the strict zero-margin fallback become authoritative with no error/severity escalation. Both the zero-margin default and the silent-degrade-on-missing-model fallback are legitimate, verifiable defects worth fixing; the fix_sketch's suggestion to raise the primary build_tracks default margin (paired with FP guardrails) is reasonable, but a fully correct fix should also address that the second-stage refinement can silently no-op (missing checkpoint) and leave a much lossier result as final without a hard failure or prominent warning in the pipeline's success/quality gates.

defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: threed/racketsport/frame_rating.py:495-514 routes any frame reasoning 'missing_expected_players' straight to tier 'human_review' / representation 'manual_review_required' unconditionally, regardless of score -- so a frame with 3 of 4 players confidently tracked still gets zero mesh and zero skeleton_preview.

defects_verified.title: Global whole-clip top-4-by-frame-count player selection permanently drops real players when BoT-SORT re-IDs them mid-clip
defects_verified.root_cause_hypothesis: _select_stable_player_ids ranks candidate raw track ids by TOTAL frame count over the ENTIRE clip and keeps only the top max_players (<=4), with no mechanism to detect/stitch that two different raw ids belong to the same real player (e.g. after a >1s occlusion causes BoT-SORT's track_buffer=30 to expire and reissue a new id). A player whose track fragments across 2-3 ids can have every fragment lose the ranking to a more persistently-tracked spectator or to another real player's longer fragment, vanishing from the whole back half (or front half) of the clip even though they were detected throughout.
defects_verified.evidence: scripts/racketsport/track.py:184,484-494; demo clip had candidate_players=10 raw ids surviving the court filter for 4 real players, extra_players_dropped=6, extra_player_frames_dropped=172; final tracks.json for that run shows per-player coverage as low as 50.3%-53.0% for 3 of 4 output players (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/after_wolv/tracks.json); configs/racketsport/botsort_reid.yaml track_buffer=30 frames (~1s) is the plausible fragmentation trigger
defects_verified.fix_sketch: This is exactly the queued NS-03.TRK lever (McByte mask cue / CAMELTrack constrained tracklets) -- replace/augment the whole-clip global ranking with per-frame or windowed Hungarian assignment among all in-court candidates, or add explicit cross-id merge-by-proximity-and-gap before ranking.

votes.reasoning:
Code and live-run evidence both check out.

1) Code citations are accurate and current (HEAD, `scripts/racketsport/track.py` unmodified per `git status`/`git diff`):
   - `_select_stable_player_ids` (lines 484-494) ranks raw track ids purely by `-len(item[1])` (total frame count over the whole clip), tie-broken by mean confidence / first timestamp / id — a single global, whole-clip ranking with zero mechanism to detect that two different raw ids are fragments of the same real person.
   - Line 184 (`selected_ids = _select_stable_player_ids(by_player, max_players=max_players)`) is exactly where this whole-clip cap is applied in `_build_raw_track_tracks`.
   - `configs/racketsport/botsort_reid.yaml:5` confirms `track_buffer: 30` (~1s at 30fps), the plausible re-ID-expiry trigger named in the hypothesis.

2) This is the real production path, not a corner case: `_resolve_id_strategy` (track.py:345-350) resolves to `"raw_track"` for any detections whose `source` isn't `"player_labels"`. `RealYOLO26BoTSORTReIDTrackingRunner` in `threed/racketsport/orchestrator.py` (lines 485-540ish, `source_mode="yolo26m_botsort_reid"`) runs YOLO26m + BoT-SORT-ReID directly and calls `build_tracks(..., id_strategy="auto")` with no intervening cross-id stitching step. A separate re-ID/global-association mechanism does exist (`threed/racketsport/player_global_association.py`, `tracking.reid_model`/`tracking.global_association_profile` in `configs/racketsport/best_stack.json`), but it is wired only into `scripts/racketsport/process_video.py`'s raw-pool path, not into the orchestrator's tracking stage — so it does not protect the cited production flow.

3) The cited demo run substantiates the numbers verbatim: `runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/after_wolv/metrics.json` shows `counts.candidate_players=10`, `counts.extra_players_dropped=6`, `counts.extra_player_frames_dropped=172`, `counts.id_strategy="raw_track"` (resolved from `"auto"`, confirming `tracked_detections.json` has `source=None`, not `"player_labels"`). `tracks.json` has exactly 4 output players (ids 19/20/21/22) with frame counts 158/151/159/237 out of `frame_count=300` (fps 30, 10s clip) — i.e. 52.7%/50.3%/53.0%/79.0% coverage, matching the claimed "50.3%-53.0% for 3 of 4 output players" precisely.

One caveat for the record (not enough to refute): I could not independently confirm from this run alone that the 6 dropped raw ids are specifically re-ID fragments of the 4 kept real players (vs. spectators/ball-kids briefly inside the zero-margin court polygon) — raw `tracked_detections.json` shows ids 1-5 fully present in frames 0-44 then replaced by ids 19-23 at frame ~55, consistent with a mid-clip re-ID break, but that alone doesn't prove which real person each fragment belongs to. The claim itself labels this a "root_cause_hypothesis," which is the correct epistemic status — the underlying algorithmic gap (global whole-clip top-k selection, no cross-id merge) is definitively real and present at the cited lines, and is a legitimate mechanism by which real players can lose coverage or be dropped whenever BoT-SORT reissues an id mid-clip.

The numeric evidence cited (candidate_players=10, extra_players_dropped=6, extra_player_frames_dropped=172, 3-of-4 output players at 50.3%-53.0% coverage) is real and I reproduced it exactly by re-running the production court_polygon_filter against runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/fresh_wolv/.../raw_tracked_detections.json with its court_calibration.json: the 10 court-filter-accepted raw ids are {22:237,21:159,19:158,20:151,3:45,4:45,2:44,6:24,55:10,15:4}; top-4 by count = 705 accepted = exactly the after_wolv/tracks.json output, and the dropped 6 (45+45+44+24+10+4=172) match extra_player_frames_dropped exactly. So `_select_stable_player_ids` (track.py:484-494) genuinely has no cross-id merge and the code-level description of that function is accurate.

However the claim is overstated/mis-attributed on two load-bearing points:

1. Wrong failure mechanism. The claim blames 'occlusion mid-clip' exceeding BoT-SORT's track_buffer=30 (~1s), causing a player to 'vanish from the whole back half (or front half) of the clip.' The artifact contradicts this: ids 2/3/4/6 (the bulk of the dropped frames, 158/172) exist only in frames 0-44 (t=0-1.47s), then ALL detections vanish for frames 45-54 (a 10-frame, 0.33s whole-frame dropout, well under the 30-frame buffer), after which ids 19/20/21/22 appear at frame 55-56 and then track with a SINGLE unbroken raw id for the remaining ~8.5 seconds of the clip (e.g. id 20/21 have exactly as many raw detections as frames in their [55,299] span — zero gaps). There is no mid-clip or back-half fragmentation event for any of the 4 real players anywhere in this demo evidence; the loss is a front-of-clip startup artifact (camera settle/scene-cut), not an in-play occlusion exceeding a 1s buffer.

2. Mis-attributed fix effort. process_video.py already implements exactly the 'cross-id merge-by-proximity' mechanism the claim's fix_sketch calls for: a ReID-based 'raw-pool global association' step (scripts/racketsport/process_video.py:1499 comment literally calls it 'the champion recipe', using osnet_x1_0_market1501.pt + motion via run_raw_pool_authority_candidate). configs/racketsport/best_stack.json marks both tracking.reid_model and tracking.global_association_profile as status=WIRED_DEFAULT (the production default), with its own notes field stating plainly: 'Local checkpoint is not staged in this checkout; the manifest records the current default selector without fabricating an available checkpoint claim.' The cited demo's PIPELINE_SUMMARY.json shows exactly this: 'raw-pool global association skipped: --reid-model .../osnet_x1_0_market1501.pt not found; kept loose-pool tracks.json' — i.e. the fix mechanism exists, is the wired default, and was skipped purely because a checkpoint file wasn't staged on that host, a condition the team already documents as known/optional. That is an asset-staging/ops gap, not an unaddressed 'no mechanism to detect/stitch' architecture hole requiring multiday new NS-03.TRK research (McByte/CAMELTrack). The claim never mentions this existing mechanism despite it being directly referenced in the very run its evidence comes from.
votes.corrected_root_cause: The naive `_select_stable_player_ids` top-4-by-total-frame-count ranking (track.py:484-494) does lack cross-id merging when running in raw_track fallback mode, but in the cited demo (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710) that fallback only fired because the WIRED_DEFAULT raw-pool ReID global-association step ('champion recipe', process_video.py:1499, configs/racketsport/best_stack.json tracking.reid_model/tracking.global_association_profile) was skipped for a documented, known reason: models/checkpoints/osnet_x1_0_market1501.pt was not staged on that host. Separately, the specific coverage loss observed (50-53% for 3 of 4 players) is a front-of-clip startup detection dropout (~10 frames / 0.33s of zero whole-frame detections around t=1.5-1.9s), not a mid-clip/back-half occlusion exceeding BoT-SORT's 30-frame track_buffer as hypothesized — all 4 real players track on a single unbroken raw id for the remaining ~8.5s of the clip. The correct, much smaller fix is staging/wiring the existing OSNet ReID checkpoint (and/or investigating any case where the buffer genuinely is exceeded by real in-play occlusion), not building new McByte/CAMELTrack constrained-tracklet capability from scratch.

Lines 832-833 confirm the 'rally_gating' stage (which produces rally_spans.json) is only appended to the prefix stage list when `self.options.rally_gating` is truthy -- otherwise the stage never runs and rally_spans.json is never written. threed/racketsport/ball_arc_chain.py:723-799 contains `_bridge_rally_span_gaps`, which immediately returns `([], [])` if `spans` (derived from `rally_spans`) is empty/None -- bridging is a strict no-op without rally_spans.json. configs/racketsport/best_stack.json has no rally_gating entry (only a single unrelated notes string mentioning 'rally' in the context of match_stats exclusions), confirming rally_gating is absent from the production/default stack. The cited demo artifact runs/lanes/demo_beststack_gpu_20260710/vm_pull/zwcth45s_r2/ball_arc_render.json was inspected directly and shows summary.rally_span_count=0 and summary.bridge_sample_count=0, with bridges=[] and every sample/segment bridge flag False -- matching the claimed observed behavior exactly. The claim is accurately scoped (notes it only closes gaps between already-trusted segments, not a full fix) and not overstated. Nothing indicates this is already fixed or mis-attributed.

Every load-bearing element of the claim is directly confirmed by the actual repo code and the real demo-clip artifacts. (1) scripts/racketsport/process_video.py:454 sets `rally_gating: bool = False` as the CliOptions default, and line 832 only appends the `rally_gating` stage function to `stage_fns` when `self.options.rally_gating` is truthy — so it is opt-in and skipped by default. (2) `rally_spans.json` is produced solely by that stage (process_video.py:399 maps `rally_gating` -> `("rally_spans.json",)`, and the stage body at line 2005/2067-2069 is the only writer of that file). Downstream code only loads it if the file exists on disk (process_video.py:3812-3817, :3989 `_read_optional_json(rally_spans_path) if rally_spans_path is not None else None`). (3) `_bridge_rally_span_gaps` (ball_arc_chain.py:723-731) short-circuits to `return [], []` whenever `spans` (derived from `rally_spans`) is empty, i.e. whenever rally_spans.json never existed — exactly the dead-code path described. (4) `configs/racketsport/best_stack.json` (rev 11, 2026-07-10) enumerates stages across all its entries (ball, ball_arc, body, cadence, calibration, camera_motion, confidence_gate, frames, input_quality, instrument, match_stats, mesh, paddle_pose, placement, product, server, tracking) and contains no `rally_gating` entry, confirming its absence from the production default. (5) The actual demo-clip artifacts corroborate the runtime consequence exactly as claimed: runs/lanes/demo_beststack_gpu_20260710/vm_pull/zwcth45s_r2/ball_arc_render.json summary shows `rally_span_count: 0` and `bridge_sample_count: 0`, while the pre-render solve artifact ball_track_arc_solved.json in the same directory shows `segment_count: 8` / `fit_segment_count: 8` (8 solved segments) — matching the claim's '0 bridges ... despite 8 solved segments' verbatim (the sibling zwcth45s directory shows an identical summary, so this is not a one-off). PIPELINE_SUMMARY.json for that same run lists the executed stage sequence (ingest, calibration, input_quality, tracking, camera_motion, placement, ball, ball_arc, events, ball_fill, frames, body, placement_refine, grounding_refine, paddle_pose, world, confidence_gate, manifest, match_stats) with no rally_gating stage present, and even notes 'no replay_scene.json written: no tight contact-window/rally point spans available' at the confidence_gate stage — direct runtime confirmation rally spans were absent on this production run. Incidental details also match code precisely: band='arc_weak', bridge=True, confidence=0.2 via `BRIDGE_CONFIDENCE = 0.2` at ball_arc_chain.py:44, render_only=True. Severity/effort/fix-sketch framing (turn rally_gating on by default or otherwise always produce rally_spans.json; note it only closes gaps between already-trusted segments) are reasonable engineering judgments consistent with the mechanism and not contradicted by anything found. No artifact contradicts the claim.

defects_medium_low.title: Body mesh windows are fetched on ~0.4s cadence with only a 2-entry LRU cache, risking visible mesh pop/stutter during network-served playback
defects_medium_low.root_cause_hypothesis: The body_mesh_index for the demo clip has 23 windows over a 10s clip (~0.43s average window length); each window crossing during forward playback issues a chunk fetch unless already cached, and the cache only retains 2 windows. On localhost this is fast, but over a real network (production) each ~0.4s window boundary is a fetch-latency risk that would visibly stall or blank the mesh.
defects_medium_low.evidence: web/replay/src/App.tsx:719-771 (fetch effect + LRU cap 2 at line 750); body_mesh_index.json window list (23 windows, computed in-session) at runs/lanes/w7_critique_20260709/wolv_world/wolverine_mixed_0200_mid_steep_corner/body_mesh_index/body_mesh_index.json
defects_medium_low.fix_sketch: Prefetch the next 1-2 windows ahead of playhead time (not just reactively on crossing), and/or widen the LRU cache; measure real network fetch latency per window against the ~0.43s window budget before deciding cache size.

facts.fact: World player frames are keyed by nearest-time lookup (frameForTime), and both the real-joint skeleton and the generic proxy stick-figure fallback require frame.joints_world or frame.floor_world_xyz/track_world_xy to be non-null. When a player has zero tracked data at a time slot (frame object exists with only `t`, all pose fields null), Player renders an empty <group> — no skeleton, no mesh, no floor dot, nothing.

facts.fact: The literal file /Users/arnavchokshi/Desktop/dinkvision_demo_20260710.mp4 (15,593,639 bytes, md5 ce5c52eec544790fbe5e4ca71ab0b242) is byte-for-byte identical to runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/final/dinkvision_demo_v3.mp4 (same md5). v1 (md5 859b4e73...) and v2 (md5 8926171e...) do NOT match.

facts.fact: v3's concat order (montage_v3.txt) + ffprobe duration (97.233333s, matches exactly the summed segment durations) establishes the absolute timeline: seg_card_title 0-5s, seg_card_2d 5-10s, seg_tracks 10-16s, seg_skeleton 16-22s, seg_ball2d 22-30s, seg_card_world 30-35s, seg_world3d 35-45.03s, seg_card_ball 45.03-51.03s, seg_ball3d_sbs 51.03-61.23s, seg_card_owner 61.23-68.23s, seg_zwcth_ball2d 68.23-76.23s, seg_card_fresh 76.23-84.23s, seg_card_week 84.23-91.23s, seg_card_end 91.23-97.23s.

Skeleton overlay is sparse colored dot clusters (no connecting bone lines/mesh), and at least one clearly visible on-court person (far-left, near-net, dark clothing) has zero skeleton dots in some sampled frames while adjacent players do.

Card 2 ('THE 3D WORLD') claims 'ghost meshes for uncertain frames instead of hiding them' -- but the sampled frames show some people with literally zero joint markers, not a faded/ghost fallback, i.e. the design intent in the card does not match what is on screen for at least this segment/these people.

facts.fact: The demo's own on-screen text quantifies the 3D-ball fail-closed tradeoff directly: seg_ball3d_sbs is a side-by-side 'BEFORE fail-open 3D ball' vs 'AFTER fail-closed (tonight)' comparison. BEFORE: 237/300 measured, 63 predicted, 0 hidden (300/300 ball positions always shown, but includes fabricated positions up to 23.5m high). AFTER (the shipped/current build): 54/300 measured, 11 predicted, 235 hidden -- i.e. 235/300 = 78.3% of frames show NO ball at all in the 3D view.

Card 3 ('TONIGHT'S FIX: 3D BALL FAILS CLOSED') states in its own words: '"0 hidden" became "235 hidden" on the same clip.'

facts.fact: Card 1 ('SEEING THE GAME') claims 2D ball coverage of 80.6% of rally frames (vs a cited 58.7% commercial benchmark) -- this is the flat/2D overlay coverage, which is roughly 3.7x higher than the 21.7% (65/300) 3D-world ball visibility rate demoed 45 seconds later in the same video. The demo's own numbers show the 2D and 3D ball pipelines have very different (and very different quality) visibility rates.
facts.evidence: seg_card_2d.mp4 on-screen text at t~5-10s vs seg_ball3d_sbs.mp4 stat header at t~51-61s, both within the same reviewed mp4.

facts.fact: In current repo code (main branch, HEAD at review time), the default process_video.py 'world' stage (threed/racketsport/orchestrator.py:2842-2858, calling build_virtual_world_state_from_files) never passes ball_track_arc_solved_path, even though that keyword parameter exists on the function (virtual_world.py:213). Downstream, apply_ball_track_arc_solved_overlay (virtual_world.py:424, called at line 129) short-circuits at line 458-459 ('if not isinstance(ball_track_arc_solved, Mapping): return ball_track_physics_filled') when that argument is None -- meaning virtual_world.json, the artifact the default pipeline produces for every clip, never gets the fail-closed ball suppression applied. The fail-closed policy is only applied inside the separate ball_arc_render.json artifact (threed/racketsport/ball_arc_chain.py:291-399, produced by the distinct 'ball_arc' pipeline stage) and inside the standalone script scripts/racketsport/rebuild_ball_trail_from_arc_solved.py.
facts.evidence: Read threed/racketsport/orchestrator.py:2842-2858, virtual_world.py:202-232 (build_virtual_world_state_from_files signature) and :454-461 (short-circuit branch), ball_arc_chain.py:291,314,397-399. Corroborated independently by runs/lanes/demo_beststack_gpu_20260710/report.json's honest_issues list, written by a separate GPU lane the same night, which states verbatim: 'apply_ball_track_arc_solved_overlay() ... is called only from the standalone script ... never from process_video.py's own "world" stage ... the NUMERIC acceptance bar (max z<5m) is met ... but the acceptance criterion's literal key-path check cannot be satisfied from a cold process_video.py run as currently wired.'

R1 failed with a remote-dispatch config gap ('--host missing'); R2 failed with FileNotFoundError 'missing BODY frame image for frame 41; expected body_frames/frame_000041.jpg' (threed/racketsport/orchestrator.py:3291-3294, function _find_body_frame_image). No skeleton3d.json/racket_pose_estimate.json/body_mesh_index exists for either zwcth45s attempt.' defects.title: 3D-world ball is hidden ~78% of frames on the primary demo clip by the fail-closed policy, and the demo frames this as a positive result
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: The fail-closed policy (threed/racketsport/virtual_world.py:371-421, ball_arc_segment_fail_closed_verdicts) correctly refuses to render an unbounded/untrusted 3D ball position rather than fabricate one -- but the underlying arc-solver's trusted-segment coverage on real (non-synthetic) rally footage is low enough that fail-closed suppression removes the ball from the overwhelming majority of frames (235/300 = 78.3% on the Wolverine demo clip; ~95.7% on the owner's own footage). The team's own demo card 3 states this outcome as the intended tradeoff ('0 hidden became 235 hidden ... only the honesty changed'), i.e. the low visibility rate is a known, accepted, and unaddressed consequence rather than an unnoticed bug -- the underlying coverage/accuracy of the arc solver was never improved to compensate.
defects.evidence: seg_ball3d_sbs.mp4 (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/final/) on-screen stat header at t=52-60s: 'AFTER fail-closed (tonight): Ball 54/300 measured * 11 predicted * 235 hidden.' Card text in seg_card_ball.mp4 ('3 . TONIGHT'S FIX: 3D BALL FAILS CLOSED'): '"0 hidden" became "235 hidden" on the same clip.' Code: threed/racketsport/virtual_world.py:519-531 (the forced_hidden_count / suppressed_count branches).
defects.fix_sketch: Improve the arc-solver's trusted-segment coverage (more inlier tolerance tuning, better 2D detection feeding it, or a lower-confidence-but-explicitly-labeled 'predicted' band shown with a visibly different render style) rather than binary hide/show, so the ball is visually present (even if flagged uncertain) far more often than 22% of frames; treat the 78% hidden rate as a product-blocking regression, not an accepted tradeoff.

defects.title: Fail-closed ball safety policy is not wired into the default pipeline artifact (virtual_world.json) that process_video.py produces for every run
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: threed/racketsport/orchestrator.py:2842-2858 calls build_virtual_world_state_from_files without ball_track_arc_solved_path, even though that parameter exists (virtual_world.py:213) and is required to trigger apply_ball_track_arc_solved_overlay's fail-closed suppression (virtual_world.py:458-459 short-circuits to the raw, unfiltered ball_track_physics_filled data when the arg is None). The fail-closed policy is only actually exercised in the separate ball_arc_render.json artifact (ball_arc_chain.py:291-399) and the standalone rebuild script. Whether this matters in practice depends on which artifact the frontend 3D viewer reads for ball position (virtual_world_url vs ball_arc_render_url, both exposed separately in replay_viewer_manifest.py:88-89) -- not resolved in this probe.
defects.evidence: threed/racketsport/orchestrator.py:2844-2854 (no ball_track_arc_solved_path argument passed); virtual_world.py:456-461 (short-circuit when arg is None); ball_arc_chain.py:291,314,397-399 (separate call site that does apply the policy). Independently corroborated by runs/lanes/demo_beststack_gpu_20260710/report.json honest_issues: 'the acceptance criterion's literal key-path check cannot be satisfied from a cold process_video.py run as currently wired.'
defects.fix_sketch: Wire ball_track_arc_solved_path through the orchestrator's world-stage call so virtual_world.json carries the same explicit fail_closed/suppressed_segment_ids attestation ball_arc_render.json already gets, OR (if by design only the render-trail artifact should carry it) update viewer code to definitively source 3D ball position from ball_arc_render.json, and document that virtual_world.json's ball_xyz is not fail-closed-filtered. Currently ambiguous which artifact is authoritative for the 3D page.

This suggests the owner's framerate complaint is most likely about the live interactive 3D replay viewer application (a separate, out-of-scope surface per this task's exclusion of runs/lanes/dr_viewer_20260710), not about this pre-rendered demo video file.

gap_findings.area: S5 paddle-bad gap-fill probe: locating and verifying a visibly mispositioned paddle in the shipped demo video

facts.fact: All three shipped demo cuts (dinkvision_demo_v1.mp4, v2.mp4, v3.mp4 in runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/final/) include a '3D page' segment seg_world3d.mp4 at global timestamp 35.0s-45.033s, sourced from the before_wolv/after_wolv (burlington/wolverine) pipeline runs, showing all 4 players with paddle overlays in the 'Broadcast' camera mode.

Verified by extracting frame directly from dinkvision_demo_v3.mp4 at t=39.5s (/tmp/paddle_probe/v3_at_39_5.png) which matches the segment-file extraction.

facts.fact: All three shipped cuts also include seg_ball3d_sbs.mp4 (before/after fail-open-vs-fail-closed 3D-ball side-by-side) at global timestamp 51.033s-61.233s, which independently renders the same 4-player paddle overlay in both the BEFORE and AFTER panels.

facts.fact: In every sampled frame of seg_world3d.mp4 and seg_ball3d_sbs.mp4 (2fps sampling across both segments, plus two direct spot-checks on the final mp4 at t=39.5s and t=55.0s -- 6 independent extraction points total), the paddle mesh (orange face-plate + blue handle) renders overlapping or embedded in the player's torso/chest or hip/pelvis, not extended away from the body at a hand. This holds for opaque ('measured') players, translucent-green ('ghost'/predicted) players, and even a frame where one player renders as a bare joint-skeleton with no body mesh at all.

The only zwcth-derived segment in any cut is seg_zwcth_ball2d.mp4, a plain 2D camera-frame video with a 2D ball-track overlay drawn on it (orange dot/line) -- no 3D mesh, no paddle mesh, no skeleton/mesh player rendering of any kind.

facts.evidence: runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/before_wolv/virtual_world.json vs racket_pose_estimate.json (compared field-for-field via python3/json in this session); web/replay/src/viewerData.ts:481,501,508,600,1084,3158-3191 (paddle read/parse code, no positional adjustment visible).

This is not VERIFIED, not canonical racket_pose.json, and not an RKT promotion.'
facts.evidence: configs/racketsport/best_stack.json:561-589 (paddle.fused_estimator entry).

facts.fact: The paddle mesh geometry is defined in threed/racketsport/paddle_pose_fused.py as a fused wrist+palm+grip-transform SE3 estimate (hand frame origin = selected wrist, grip transform G = hand->paddle held constant per grip segment); the module's own docstring frames this as render-continuity-only, not true 6-DoF paddle pose.
facts.evidence: threed/racketsport/paddle_pose_fused.py lines 1-95 (module docstring, SOURCE='wrist_palm_grip_fused', BAND_PALM_FITTED/BAND_GRIP_EXTRAPOLATED constants).

defects.title: Paddle mesh renders but is torso/pelvis-clipped, not hand-anchored, throughout both '3D page' demo segments (~20s / ~21% of shipped runtime)
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: The wrist+palm+grip-transform fused paddle estimator (threed/racketsport/paddle_pose_fused.py, source=wrist_palm_grip_fused) computes a paddle pose_se3.t that lands near the player's torso/pelvis centerline instead of at the extended hand/grip point. All frames in this clip fall in the lowest-fidelity 'palm_fitted' band (0 contact_locked, 0 grip_extrapolated), and the resulting pose is passed through unmodified via virtual_world.json to the viewer (confirmed identical pose_se3 in both files), so the defect originates in the estimator/hand-frame-solve stage, not in viewer-side rendering/transform code. The estimator is explicitly self-flagged as render_only/never_canonical/preview, yet best_stack.json wires it in with enabled=true and it is drawn as an opaque, confident-looking solid paddle plate with no visual indication of its low trust band.
defects.evidence: dinkvision_demo_v1.mp4/v2.mp4/v3.mp4 (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/final/) at t=35.0-45.033s (seg_world3d.mp4) and t=51.033-61.233s (seg_ball3d_sbs.mp4); direct spot-checks at t=39.5s and t=55.0s on the shipped v3.mp4 reproduce the same artifact. Screenshots: /tmp/paddle_probe/crop_fl.png, crop_fr.png, crop_010_front.png, crop_010_back.png, world3d_015_full4.png, v3_at_39_5.png, v3_at_55_0.png. Source data: runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/before_wolv/racket_pose_estimate.json (status=preview, render_only=true, trust=estimated_from_wrist, band_distribution 100% palm_fitted of 705 frames); configs/racketsport/best_stack.json:561-589.
defects.fix_sketch: Short-term (hours): gate the paddle overlay's visual confidence to match its actual trust_band -- e.g. render palm_fitted-band paddles as a dashed/translucent outline (matching the existing 'low_confidence' display_band already present in confidence_provenance) instead of an opaque solid plate, so it doesn't read as a confidently-placed but wrong paddle. Medium-term (multiday): fix the hand-frame/grip-transform solve in paddle_pose_fused.py to anchor near the actual wrist joint -- cross-check pose_se3.t against the corresponding player's wrist joint in skeleton3d.json per frame, reject/clamp poses whose distance from the wrist exceeds ~1 paddle length, and add a regression test asserting this invariant so a future render never re-introduces torso-clipping.

defects.title: S5 reconciliation: 'paddle never renders' (zwcth clip) and 'paddle renders but mispositioned' (burlington/wolverine clips) are both true but describe different clips/segments -- not a contradiction
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: N/A -- this is a bookkeeping/reconciliation finding, not a code defect. The zwcth clip's 3D-world render (with its own racket_pose_estimate.json/virtual_world.json in runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/zwcth_world/) was never composited into any of the three shipped montage cuts; only its 2D ball-overlay (seg_zwcth_ball2d.mp4) shipped. The owner's stated symptom ('the paddle looks really bad in the 3D page ... positioning-wise') matches the burlington/wolverine-sourced seg_world3d.mp4/seg_ball3d_sbs.mp4 segments that WERE shipped and DO show a visible, badly-placed paddle -- so the owner's complaint has a genuine, verified, on-screen referent; it just isn't the same failure mode as the earlier zero-frame finding.
defects.evidence: montage_v1.txt/v2.txt/v3.txt reference only seg_zwcth_ball2d.mp4 for the zwcth clip (no 3D-world segment); runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/zwcth_world/*.json exists on disk but is orphaned from the final render outputs.
defects.fix_sketch: When cataloguing S5 findings going forward, tag each finding with clip_id + segment_file + timestamp range so 'paddle fails one way in clip A' and 'paddle fails a different way in clip B' don't get merged into one apparently-contradictory line item.

open_questions: Does the same torso/pelvis-clipping defect also appear in the 'Behind', 'Top', or dedicated 'Paddles' camera modes (buttons visible in the UI but never exercised in the shipped demo, which stays on 'Broadcast' throughout)? Not testable from the static mp4 alone -- would need a live viewer capture across camera modes.

What is the exact numeric offset (in meters) between paddle pose_se3.t and the corresponding player's true wrist joint per frame? Not computed in this probe because skeleton3d.json's joint_names are unlabeled generic identifiers (sam3dbody_joint_000..NNN) with no wrist-index mapping readily available; a follow-up agent should locate the MHR70/joint-index mapping referenced in paddle_pose_fused.py's docstring to compute this precisely for a root-cause-grade fix.

Is this same torso-clipping defect present in every processed clip that uses the fused wrist+palm+grip paddle estimator (100% palm_fitted band), or only in clips/camera-occlusion regimes where the finger/palm keypoints are degenerate? The demo's before_wolv run happened to be 100% palm_fitted (0% grip_extrapolated/contact_locked) -- worth checking whether other clips reach the higher-fidelity bands and, if so, whether those bands render correctly.

Was 'dinkvision_demo_20260710.mp4' (the exact filename the owner watched, per the mission brief) literally one of v1/v2/v3.mp4, or a renamed/re-exported copy? File search found no exact-name match in the repo; v3.mp4 is the newest/most complete cut and was used for this probe's direct spot-checks, but this identity was not independently confirmed against a manifest or delivery log.

gap_findings.area: iOS native app 3D/AR rendering surface (ios/App, ios/Replay, ios/FastTier) — gap-fill probe for whether S2-S5 defects are web-viewer-only or also apply to the shipped native surface

facts.fact: The iOS app has its own fully native 3D renderer (ios/Replay Swift package) — it does NOT embed the web viewer via WKWebView/UIWebView. grep for WKWebView/WebView/UIWebView/loadHTMLString/loadFileURL across all of ios/ returns zero matches.
facts.evidence: grep -rn "WKWebView|WebView|UIWebView|loadHTMLString|loadFileURL" ios/ --include=*.swift -> no output. File inventory: ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/{WorldViewerView,WorldSceneBuilder,WorldSceneGeometry,WorldSkeletonBones,WorldBallRendering,WorldTierSelection,VirtualWorld,BodyMesh,TrustBand,ContactWindows,WorldCameraPresets}.swift

facts.fact: The native Swift World* files are deliberate line-by-line ports/mirrors of the web viewer's TypeScript logic — same JSON schema (virtual_world.json, body_mesh.json, contact_windows.json, replay_viewer_manifest.json), each explicitly documented as mirroring a specific web/replay/src function (ballRenderInfoForTime, frameForTime, ballFrameForTime, cameraPresetPose, bodyMeshOpacityFromBlendWeight, etc.).
facts.evidence: ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/WorldBallRendering.swift:3-11 ("Mirrors ballRenderInfoForTime in web/replay/src/viewerData.ts"); WorldTierSelection.swift:16-17,28-29,44-47; WorldCameraPresets.swift:5,74; BodyMesh.swift:9,99,133; TrustBand.swift:3,13

facts.fact: iOS's native player render-tier logic (WorldFrameSelection.tier) implements an explicit fail-closed cascade equivalent to the web viewer's: MESH (only if a body_mesh frame is near AND a contact window is active) -> JOINTS (if BODY joint data covers the time) -> bare floor TRACK dot -> none, choosing exactly one tier per player per frame, never blended.
facts.evidence: ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/WorldTierSelection.swift:3-12,61-80

facts.fact: iOS's native ball-render logic (WorldBallRendering.renderInfo) has its own explicit 4-state mode (missing / calibrated3D / courtPlaneProjection / offCourtProjection) driven purely from per-frame virtual_world.json ball.frames[].world_xyz — it does not fabricate a position when world_xyz is nil.
facts.evidence: ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/WorldBallRendering.swift:12-46

facts.fact: The canonical manifest schema (server-side, shared source of truth) declares an optional ball_arc_render_url field carrying a dense, gap-filled/interpolated ball trail (segments/bridges/samples, up to 0.25s gap interpolation) produced by a separate ball-arc solver — this is the mechanism the web viewer uses to reduce perceived ball-hidden time (it swaps in ballArcRenderSamples in place of raw per-frame ball data whenever present).
facts.evidence: threed/racketsport/schemas/__init__.py:1413,2019 (ball_arc_render_url); web/replay/src/viewerData.ts:80,682-683; web/replay/src/App.tsx:16,516,631-632,653,913-919,1264-1265 (world={ballArcRenderSamples.length?null:world}, samples={ballArcRenderSamples.length?ballArcRenderSamples:null}); web/replay/src/ballArcRender.ts:95-128 (sampleBallArcRenderAtTime, maxGapSeconds=0.25)

facts.fact: iOS's WorldViewerManifest Codable struct has NO field/CodingKey for ball_arc_render_url — only virtualWorldURL, bodyMeshURL, physicsRefinementURL, contactWindowsURL, ballInflectionsURL are declared and decoded. A JSONDecoder silently ignores unrecognized keys, so even if the real manifest.json contains ball_arc_render_url, iOS structurally cannot load or use it.
facts.evidence: ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/WorldViewerManifest.swift:8-31 (CodingKeys enum has no ballArcRenderURL case); confirmed by repo-wide grep -rln "ball_arc_render" showing zero hits under ios/

facts.fact: iOS has no arc-fitting/interpolation/gap-filling logic anywhere in its ball-rendering path — grep for "interpolat" under ios/ only matches two unrelated UI-animation files (DinkVisionDesignSystem.swift, AppRootView.swift), neither in the Replay/World ball-rendering code.
facts.evidence: grep -rln interpolat ios/ --include=*.swift -> ios/App/DinkVisionDesignSystem.swift, ios/App/AppRootView.swift only

facts.fact: The shipped iOS app's actual replay-playback screen (reached by tapping a real capture row in the local library) ALWAYS loads the bundled fixture sample via WorldBundle.loadBundledSample() — never a manifest tied to the user's own capture. The on-screen caption literally states this: "Existing replay module. Bundled sample until this capture has server output."
facts.evidence: ios/App/AppRootView.swift:1631-1704, specifically line 1699 `loadedBundle = try WorldBundle.loadBundledSample()` and line 1675 caption text; the fixture is Bundle.module's Resources/WorldFixture/replay_viewer_manifest.json per ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/WorldBundle.swift:44-55

facts.fact: The local capture-library row model (DinkVisionReplayRow, sourced from CaptureLibraryItem) carries no field referencing a server-produced replay_viewer_manifest.json / job result at all — there is no plumbing from a completed capture to a per-clip 3D artifact in the row data itself.
facts.evidence: ios/App/DinkVisionModels.swift:486-524 (DinkVisionReplayRow fields: id, title, subtitle, durationText, dateText, trustBadgeText, trusted3DText, ballTrustText, item: CaptureLibraryItem — no manifestURL/jobID/replayManifest field)

facts.fact: grep across the entire App target (ios/App/*.swift) and Upload package (ios/Upload/Sources/PickleballUpload/*.swift) for WorldBundle finds exactly one construction site total (the fixture-only one above) — there is no code path anywhere in the shipped app that constructs a WorldBundle from a real, server-returned manifest URL.
facts.evidence: grep -rn WorldBundle ios/App/*.swift ios/AppTests/*.swift ios/AppUITests/*.swift -> only ios/App/AppRootView.swift:1635 (declaration) and :1699 (loadBundledSample call)

facts.fact: RenderGatewayClient.swift defines a replayURL(for job:) helper that builds a URL in the exact query-param convention the WEB viewer reads (?manifest=<serverManifestURL>, matching web/replay/src/App.tsx:304's `new URLSearchParams(search).get("manifest")`), implying an intended after-upload handoff to the web viewer for real per-job replays — but this function has zero callers anywhere in ios/ (no UIApplication.shared.open, no SFSafariViewController, nothing invokes it). It is dead/unwired scaffolding.
facts.evidence: ios/Upload/Sources/PickleballUpload/RenderGatewayClient.swift:223-233; grep -rn "replayURL(for" ios/ --include=*.swift -> only the definition; grep -rn "replayURL|openURL|SFSafari|UIApplication.shared.open" ios/App/*.swift ios/Upload/Sources/PickleballUpload/*.swift -> only the same definition line; web/replay/src/App.tsx:304,310 confirms the `manifest` query-param convention exists on the web side

facts.fact: Net effect: after a real capture is recorded, uploaded, and processed by the server pipeline on the shipped iOS app, there is currently NO code path (native or web-handoff) that shows the user their actual processed 3D replay — the only reachable 3D content in the app is the static bundled Burlington-derived fixture.
facts.evidence: Composite of the WorldBundle-construction-site fact, DinkVisionReplayRow field fact, and dead replayURL(for:) fact above; corroborated by DinkVisionUploadCoordinator.swift containing zero references to WorldBundle/replay_viewer_manifest (grep -n "replay_viewer_manifest|WorldBundle|manifest" ios/App/DinkVisionUploadCoordinator.swift -> no output)

facts.fact: ios/AppUITests/WorldViewerUITests.swift (the device-smoke test for the native 3D viewer) looks for a home-screen button labeled "View 3D World" (app.buttons["View 3D World"]), but that exact string does not appear anywhere in ios/App/AppRootView.swift or elsewhere in ios/App — the only reachable entry to 3D content in current source is per-row tap (accessibilityIdentifier "DinkVisionReplayRow-<id>") into DinkVisionReplayPlaybackScreen.
facts.evidence: ios/AppUITests/WorldViewerUITests.swift:1-24 (`let openWorldViewer = app.buttons["View 3D World"]`); grep -rn "View 3D World" ios/App/*.swift -> no output; grep -n "Button(" ios/App/AppRootView.swift | grep -i world|replay|open -> only the row's Button(action: onOpen) at line 1465

facts.fact: The North Star roadmap treats ios/ as a first-class, non-experimental product surface ("Native capture/live/upload/replay surfaces | ios/") and lists REPLAY/STATS gate criteria requiring native/web visual+perf parity proof, which per current evidence is not met since native never renders real per-capture content.
facts.evidence: NORTH_STAR_ROADMAP.md:25, 174; NS-01.2b (line ~300) still listed as an open gate per memory (Prove physical upload / real device record-import-upload-job-artifact-replay trace)

defects.title: iOS replay-playback screen is hardcoded to the bundled fixture sample — never loads real per-capture pipeline output (no manifest plumbing exists at all)
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: DinkVisionReplayPlaybackScreen unconditionally calls WorldBundle.loadBundledSample() regardless of which capture row was tapped; DinkVisionReplayRow (from the local capture library) has no field for a server job/manifest URL, and RenderGatewayClient's replayURL(for:)/manifestUrl plumbing that would carry a real manifest reference from a completed job is never wired to any UI. This appears to be intentional, labeled scaffolding ("Bundled sample until this capture has server output") that was never completed, not a regression.
defects.evidence: ios/App/AppRootView.swift:1631-1704 (line 1699: loadedBundle = try WorldBundle.loadBundledSample(); line 1675 caption); ios/App/DinkVisionModels.swift:486-524 (no manifest/job field on the row); ios/Upload/Sources/PickleballUpload/RenderGatewayClient.swift:223-233 (replayURL(for:) has 0 callers, grep confirms); ios/App/DinkVisionUploadCoordinator.swift has 0 references to WorldBundle/replay_viewer_manifest
defects.fix_sketch: Thread the completed job's manifestUrl (already returned by RenderGatewayJob.result?.manifestUrl per RenderGatewayClient.swift:142,148) through to DinkVisionReplayRow, download/cache the manifest+artifacts to Documents, and call WorldBundle.load(manifestURL:) instead of loadBundledSample() whenever real server output exists for that row; keep the fixture only as an explicit empty-state fallback.

defects.title: iOS manifest schema is missing the ball_arc_render_url field, so the native viewer cannot consume the dense/gap-filled ball trail artifact even once real-manifest loading is wired
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: WorldViewerManifest.swift's Codable/CodingKeys enum was written to mirror an earlier/partial version of the manifest schema and was never updated when ball_arc_render_url was added to the canonical schema (threed/racketsport/schemas/__init__.py:2019) and wired into the web viewer's rendering path (App.tsx:1264-1265). Because Swift's JSONDecoder silently drops unknown keys, this fails silently rather than erroring.
defects.evidence: ios/Replay/Sources/PickleballReplay/World/WorldViewerManifest.swift:8-31 (no ballArcRenderURL case); threed/racketsport/schemas/__init__.py:2019; web/replay/src/App.tsx:631-632,1264-1265; web/replay/src/ballArcRender.ts:95-128; zero hits for "ball_arc_render" anywhere under ios/
defects.fix_sketch: Add ballArcRenderURL (CodingKey "ball_arc_render_url") to WorldViewerManifest, load+decode ball_arc_render.json in WorldBundle.load, and port sampleBallArcRenderAtTime's interpolation/gap-fill logic (ballArcRender.ts:95-128) into a Swift equivalent consumed by WorldBallRendering so the native ball tier gets the same gap-filled trail the web viewer gets.

defects.title: WorldViewerUITests.swift's device-smoke test targets a "View 3D World" home-screen button that no longer exists in ios/App source — the test is stale relative to the shipped app and would fail (or already fails) on physical-device gate runs

facts.fact: Across the repo's history, headless-captured '3D FPS' readings for the world view swing wildly by clip/manifest -- from 3.0-6.0 (body_chunkfix_verify, speed1_wolverine, visual_polish, w4_freshproof/wolverine) up to 41-79 (process_audit_2, placement_stage, manager_stage_sam3d_wolverine, world_precedence) -- using the identical headless=True capture method, i.e. the number is not a stable/fixed rendering cost even before considering headless-vs-real-GPU.

facts.fact: Live probe (this session): loading the identical manifest/URL/app code in a real (non-headless) Playwright Chromium reports WebGL renderer 'ANGLE (Apple, ANGLE Metal Renderer: Apple M1 Pro...)' -- real GPU hardware compositing -- and FpsProbe reads 95.9-116.1fps after a ~1s warm-up transient (two independent runs, mean ~100 and ~95, after dropping the first sample each run).
facts.evidence: output of /tmp/s1_fps_probe.py (this session), mode headed_real_gpu, gpu_info.renderer, raw_samples

facts.fact: This ~20x headless-vs-headed gap reproduces both against a stale worktree checkout (/private/tmp/critique_viewer_worktree, git HEAD 89858524, 2026-07-07) that a leftover 'dr_viewer_20260710'-adjacent dev server on port 5173 happened to be serving, and against the current main HEAD (3cfede00b, 2026-07-10, served from a separate dev server on port 5199) -- diffing App.tsx between the two checkouts shows the FpsProbe/updateFpsSample code is byte-identical; only unrelated body-mesh trust-badge material logic differs. facts.evidence: diff of web/replay/src/App.tsx vs /private/tmp/critique_viewer_worktree/web/replay/src/App.tsx (40-line diff, no FpsProbe/useFrame hunk); re-run of /tmp/s1_fps_probe.py against port 5199 (current HEAD) reproduced headless mean 4.86fps, headed mean ~95-100fps

facts.fact: CDP Performance.getMetrics deltas over a fixed 5s window show: headless -- main-thread TaskDuration ~5.01s of 5.03s wall (main thread ~100% saturated) of which only ScriptDuration ~1.05-1.06s (~21%); headed/real-GPU -- TaskDuration ~4.1s of 5.03s wall (~82% busy) of which ScriptDuration is ~3.87-3.97s (~96-97% of task time). Because the headed run executes ~20x more frames in the same wall time, per-frame script cost computed from these numbers is ~45ms/frame in headless vs ~7-8ms/frame in headed/real-GPU.
facts.evidence: output of /tmp/s1_cdp_breakdown.py (this session), both the stale-worktree (port 5173) and current-HEAD (port 5199) runs, keys TaskDuration/ScriptDuration/wall_elapsed_s

facts.fact: Interpretation (derived from the two facts above, not a repo artifact): of the low-single-digit headless '3D FPS' reading, the large majority (roughly 80-95% depending on which metric is used) is non-script overhead attributable to SwiftShader software WebGL rasterization contending with the main thread, not genuine React/Three.js per-frame computation; the genuine app-logic cost (solidBodyMeshFramesForTime + alignBodyMeshFrameToWorldSkeleton + React/R3F reconciliation) measured under real GPU compositing is small (~7-8ms/frame), comfortably inside a 30fps (33ms) or even 60-120fps budget.
facts.evidence: derived from CDP TaskDuration/ScriptDuration deltas and FpsProbe frame counts above (this session's probe, not a pre-existing repo document)
