defects.title: Demo-video world panel is exported at 10 unique frames/sec (screenshot-step capture) then frame-duplicated into a 30fps container
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: The demo render lane (runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710) captures the 'world' 3D-viewer panel by taking discrete Playwright headless screenshots at a fixed 0.1s step (10 samples/sec) instead of continuously recording the live render loop, then ffmpeg-concats those stills with duration=0.1 and encodes at -r 10, and that 10fps clip is later composited into a 30fps-container final video -- so the owner is watching real, structural judder in the world-panel segments (~10 unique frames/sec, tripled/duplicated to fill a 30fps container), independent of anything the live app's own render loop is doing.
defects.evidence: runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/segments/notes.md:12-14,23-24; runs/lanes/demo_beststack_render_20260710/make_concat_list.py (--step 0.1 default, concat duration=frame_duration); ffprobe on world_after_cleancrop.mp4 (102 frames/10.00s) and final/seg_world3d.mp4 (301 frames/10.033s=30fps container) and final/dinkvision_demo_v3.mp4 (2917 frames/97.233s=30fps)
defects.fix_sketch: Replace the 0.1s-step screenshot-loop capture with a continuous capture method for the world panel -- e.g. Playwright's built-in page video recording (`record_video_dir`), or CDP `Page.startScreencast`, or driving the real (non-headless, GPU-accelerated) browser and recording its output at >=24-30fps natively -- so the exported clip's native frame rate matches (or exceeds) its container's declared frame rate instead of being duplicated up from 10fps.

defects.title: Every repo-wide headless '3D FPS' verification number is dominated by SwiftShader software-WebGL rasterization overhead, not real app performance, and is used unlabeled across lane reports/gates
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: scripts/racketsport/verify_process_video_viewer.py:331 launches Chromium with headless=True and no GPU/ANGLE flags, so Chromium falls back to the SwiftShader software rasterizer for WebGL (confirmed renderer string 'ANGLE (Google, Vulkan ... SwiftShader Device ...)'). The identical manifest/app code rendered in a real GPU-accelerated browser (ANGLE Metal Renderer: Apple M1 Pro) is ~20x faster (~95-116fps vs ~4.5-5.5fps), and CDP Performance.getMetrics shows the headless run's main thread is ~100% busy with only ~21% of that in actual script (the rest is raster/GPU-adjacent overhead), vs ~82% busy / ~96% script under real GPU. Every historical headless_verify.json '3D FPS' figure across the repo (2.2 up to 79, wildly clip-dependent) inherits this same software-rasterization tax, so any lane/gate reasoning (e.g. the W3-SCRUBBER-V0 gate referenced in the FpsProbe docstring, or the owner's own impression from a headless-rendered demo) that treats these numbers as representative product/render performance is measuring the capture environment, not the app.
defects.evidence: scripts/racketsport/verify_process_video_viewer.py:331; web/replay/src/App.tsx:1668-1688 (FpsProbe docstring citing the W3-SCRUBBER-V0 gate); this session's live probe (gpu_info.renderer per mode, raw_samples, CDP TaskDuration/ScriptDuration deltas) run twice (stale worktree + current main HEAD) with consistent ~20x gap
defects.fix_sketch: Do not treat raw headless '3D FPS' as a performance signal without a capture-environment caveat; add a second, explicitly-labeled headed/GPU-accelerated (or headless=new with forced ANGLE/Metal/Vulkan hardware backend, e.g. `--use-angle=metal` / avoiding `--disable-gpu`) FPS smoke as the number that actually represents user-perceived render performance, and keep the existing headless check purely as a load/assertion smoke test.

defects.title: No real-device (mobile Safari/WKWebView) FPS measurement exists anywhere in the evidence surveyed -- the actual target runtime for S1 is untested
defects.root_cause_hypothesis: All FPS evidence found (headless SwiftShader ~2-79fps depending on clip, and this session's real desktop-GPU ~95-116fps) comes from a desktop Chromium (Mac M1 Pro) dev-server load, not the product's actual delivery surface implied by the North Star (native/mobile viewing). Neither number bounds what the owner's actual phone/browser will show; the ~100fps desktop-GPU figure demonstrates the app is not intrinsically compute-bound, but does not demonstrate mobile-Safari/WKWebView parity (different WebGL driver stack, thermal throttling, lower-power GPU, potentially different React/Three.js JIT behavior).
defects.evidence: absence: no mobile/WKWebView FPS artifact found in any headless_verify.json or lane report surveyed; NORTH_STAR_ROADMAP.md:174 (REPLAY/STATS row) names 'prior proof had low FPS' as a known gap with 'native/web visual/perf/every-URL proof' still the target, not yet delivered
defects.fix_sketch: Add a real-device (or at minimum mobile-Safari-via-real-hardware or BrowserStack-class) FPS capture of the same manifest/clip alongside the desktop headless and desktop-headed numbers gathered here, before concluding the framerate question is closed.

open_questions: The mission's cited '1.3-3.5fps' headless number was not found verbatim in any non-blocked artifact; the closest in-repo citation for the exact demo_beststack_render_20260710/before_wolv+after_wolv manifest is 2.2-3.3fps (viewer_smoke*, frames_before_world). The literal 1.3 endpoint may live in one of the blocked concurrent lanes (dr_viewer_20260710/dr_pipeline_20260710/dr_sota_20260710) -- worth reconciling once those lanes land, though this session's own live reproduction (4.5-5.5fps headless on the same manifest) is directionally consistent and does not change the conclusion.

Whether the demo video the owner actually watched is final/dinkvision_demo_v3.mp4 (this session's best match) or some other renamed/copied artifact (dinkvision_demo_20260710.mp4 itself was not found in the non-blocked tree) -- confirm the exact file before treating the 10fps-world-panel finding as fully dispositive for what the owner saw.

No mobile/real-device (WKWebView / mobile Safari) FPS measurement exists in any surveyed evidence; this probe only bounds desktop Chromium (software vs. real M1 Metal GPU). The product's actual target runtime performance for S1 remains unmeasured.

The elevated per-frame script cost measured in headless mode (~45ms/frame) vs headed/real-GPU (~7-8ms/frame) is only partially explained (SwiftShader contention/GC pressure hypothesized); no trace-event-level (per-call) breakdown of solidBodyMeshFramesForTime/alignBodyMeshFrameToWorldSkeleton was captured in this session (only aggregate CDP Performance.getMetrics deltas), so a finer per-function attribution is still open if deeper optimization work is scoped.

The 2 console 404 errors observed once in headed mode (stale worktree) but not reproduced against current HEAD were not isolated to a specific resource URL; if they recur they may be worth a dedicated follow-up, but are not currently a confirmed defect.
